questions.prompt: A healthy eval suite should score in roughly what range?

choices.text: 70–90%. High enough to track meaningful improvements, low enough to have room for real regressions to show up. 100% suites have stopped doing work — add harder cases or sample real production failures to keep the suite informative.

questions.explanation: Evals at 100% mean the model has out-grown the test set; you're not measuring anything anymore. Healthy evals live in the 70–90% range so regressions and improvements both have headroom to register. Rotate easy cases out and pull in real production failures as new cases.

questions.prompt: Your team is using a small/fast model as the LLM-as-judge. Scores look great (avg 4.5/5). Human spot-checks rate the same outputs 3.2/5. What's wrong?

choices.text: Small judge models tend to be lenient. Switch to Sonnet or Opus for judging and tighten the rubric. Before trusting any judge, calibrate against ~30 human-graded samples — if agreement is below 80%, fix the rubric. Once calibrated, the judge scales cheaply.

questions.explanation: Judges need calibration like any measurement instrument. Use a capable model (Sonnet or Opus), write a clear rubric, and verify against human ratings on a sample. Without calibration, the score is decorative — you don't know whether higher is actually better.
